data.title: Iran conflict latest: Trump says ’don’t care’ if Tehran returns to negotiate
data.description:
Investing.com- U.S. President Donald Trump said on Sunday evening he did not care if Iran came back to the negotiating table after weekend ceasefire talks largely fell through. 
Trump confirmed that the U.S. plans to blockade the Strait of Hormuz from Monday morning, claiming that Iran had broken its promise to reopen the key shipping channel. 
"I don’t care if they come back or not, if they don’t come back, we’re fine," Trump told reporters at Joint Base Andrews. 
[[pro_promotion |Get latest news on the Iran war by upgrading to InvestingPro]]
Trump’s comments came shortly after the Wall Street Journal reported that several countries were working to bring the U.S. and Iran back to the negotiating table after marathon talks in Islamabad ended without an agreement. 
The report said the door remains open to further diplomacy, with a second round of talks potentially possible within days. Regional governments are also in consultations with Washington to extend a fragile two-week ceasefire announced late Tuesday.
The Islamabad meeting marked the highest-level face-to-face contact between U.S. and Iranian officials since 1979. Saturday saw 21 hours of direct U.S.-Iran talks in Islamabad that ended without a deal. Vice President JD Vance said Washington had made its terms clear but Tehran had refused to accept them
The Journal reported earlier on Sunday that U.S. demands included a full end to Iran’s uranium enrichment, the dismantling of major nuclear facilities, the handover of highly enriched material, the reopening of Hormuz without tolls, broader regional peace, and an end to support for proxy groups including Hezbollah and the Houthis.
Iranian officials proposed allowing limited enrichment or reducing their stockpile of enriched uranium, but the sides failed to bridge differences, reports showed. 
Iranian Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf declared Sunday that Iran will not yield to threats, following President Trump’s announcement of a U.S. blockade on the Strait of Hormuz. Addressing Trump directly on social media, Qalibaf stated, “If you fight, we will fight."
The U.S. will begin enforcing a blockade on maritime traffic entering and leaving Iranian ports from 10 AM ET on April 13. U.S. Central Command said the blockade would apply to vessels of all nations calling at Iranian ports and coastal areas, including facilities along the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. 
It was not immediately clear whether the U.S.’ allies would be involved in blockading Hormuz. Speaking to reporters on Sunday evening, Trump criticized NATO for not helping with Iran, and said that Washington was seriously examining its relationship with the organization. 
Additional reporting by Ambar Warrick

data.title: RBC cuts ABF to “underperform” as Primark weakens, sees downside ahead
data.description:
Investing.com - RBC Capital Markets downgraded Associated British Foods Plc (LON:ABF)to “underperform” from “sector perform” on Monday, slashing its price target to 1,850p from 2,050p as Primark’s competitive position erodes and earnings forecasts fall below consensus across the group’s key divisions.
ABF shares were trading at 1,920p at the time of the report, putting the new price target 3.6% below the current price.
[[pro_promotion |
Access faster breaking news and analyst reactions with InvestingPro - up to 50% off
]]
The bank cut its FY26-28 adjusted profit before tax forecasts by 4-7%, placing its estimates 2-10% below Visible Alpha consensus for the same period. 
Adjusted EPS estimates were lowered to 157p for FY26 and 157.8p for FY27, from prior estimates of 158.2p and 164.7p respectively, according to the RBC report.
"We think ABF’s valuation is full given more limited growth prospects over the next few years," the brokerage said.
Primark, which accounts for 49% of group FY25 sales and 60% of group EBIT, is at the centre of the downgrade. 
RBC cut its forecast for the retailer’s like-for-like sales, now expecting a 2.5% decline in FY26 compared with its earlier estimate of a 1.3% drop. 
The brokerage said operating margin is likely to remain at 10% in FY26 before easing to 9.5% in FY27, while sales per square foot are projected to fall from £495.9 in FY25 to £474.8 by FY27.
Shein’s pricing is emerging as a key pressure point, RBC said, with its surveys showing the fast-fashion retailer is now cheaper than Primark in Spain and Germany, markets that account for about 13% and 7% of Primark’s sales, respectively. 
In the UK, Shein’s prices are close to parity with Primark at full price and are likely lower once promotions are factored in, the brokerage added.
Shein’s European market share gains accelerated in 2025 after its US growth stalled following the elimination of de minimis rules on direct imports from China, per Euromonitor data cited in the report.
Primark’s gross margin of about 40% leaves it more exposed than peers to rising freight and oil-linked raw material costs, RBC said.
On the food side, Grocery operating profit is forecast at £456 million for FY26, down from a prior estimate of £460 million, with margins falling to 10.8% from 11%. 
The pressure stems from price normalisation in US cooking oils under the Mazola brand and at Stratas Foods, ABF’s 50%-owned US packaged oils supplier.
Ingredients operating profit was cut to £242 million from £251 million for FY26. Sugar operating profit is forecast at just £10 million for FY26, improving to £50 million in FY27.
RBC said its price target is based on an average of discounted cash flow and sum-of-the-parts valuations. 
The DCF, which assumes a 7.5% weighted average cost of capital and a 2% terminal growth rate, implies a share price of about £17.70. 
Its SOTP valuation applies a 10 times FY27 earnings multiple to Primark, broadly in line with M&S and below NEXT’s 15 times, implying around £18.86 per share.

data.title: BOJ’s Ueda calls for vigilance against impact of Middle East war
data.description: By Leika Kihara TOKYO, April 13 (Reuters) - Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda said on Monday economic and price developments were moving roughly in line with the bank’s forecasts, but called for vigilance to the impact of escalating conflict in the Middle East. "Global financial markets are unstable and crude oil prices are rising sharply due to Middle East tensions. We must be vigilant to future developments," Ueda was quoted in a speech read out by his deputy, Ryozo Himino. The speech was closely watched by markets for any hints on whether the BOJ would raise interest rates at its next policy meeting on April 27-28 nL4N40T1P9, as fading hopes of an early end to the Iran war keep markets volatile and muddle the economic outlook. In his speech, Ueda said a gradual economic recovery was keeping underlying inflation on track to hit the BOJ’s 2% target with companies offering solid pay increases in this year’s wage negotiations. But he said rising crude oil prices would hurt Japan’s economy, adding that a protracted Middle East war could weigh on factory output amid supply chain disruptions. While higher oil costs would push up energy prices in the short-term, it could exert both upward and downward pressures on underlying inflation, he said. "If the output gap worsens, that could weigh on underlying inflation. On the other hand, if rising crude oil prices heighten the public’s medium- and long-term inflation expectations, that could push up underlying inflation," Ueda said. "Give lingering uncertainty over the Middle East situation, we will scrutinise how future developments affect the economy, prices and financial conditions, as well as risks and likelihood of our baseline projections materialising," Ueda said on the outlook for monetary policy. The reference to Middle East uncertainty marks a shift from March’s guidance, when the BOJ said only that it would continue raising rates in line with improvements in the economy and prices.

data.title: World Bank chief sounds alarm about looming jobs crisis even after war ends
data.description: By Andrea Shalal WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The Middle East war will dominate global finance officials’ talks this week in Washington, but World Bank President Ajay Banga is sounding the alarm about a bigger, looming crisis: a huge gap in jobs for the 1.2 billion people who will reach working age in developing countries in the next 10 to 15 years. At current trajectories, those economies will generate only about 400 million jobs, leaving a deficit of 800 million jobs, Banga told Reuters. The former Mastercard CEO admits that focusing people on the long-term is daunting, given a series of short-term shocks that have buffeted the global economy since the COVID-19 pandemic, the most recent being the war in the Middle East. He says he’s determined to ensure that finance officials stay focused on those longer-term challenges like creating jobs, connecting people to the electricity grid and ensuring access to clean water. "We have to walk and chew gum at the same time. Short-velocity cycle is what we’re going through. Longer velocity is this jobs circumstance or water," Banga said in an interview taped on Friday. WAR OVERSHADOWS OTHER CONCERNS Thousands of finance officials from around the globe will gather in Washington this week for the spring meetings of the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund under the shadow of the U.S.-Israel war with Iran nL6N40S1FU that threatens to slow global growth and jack up inflation nL1N40S0MH. The extent of the hit to the economy will depend on the durability of a two-week ceasefire announced by President Donald Trump https://www.reuters.com/world/us/donald-trump/ last week, just hours before promised strikes that Trump said would destroy Iran’s civilization. The ceasefire has halted most attacks. But it has not ended Iran’s effective blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, which has caused the biggest-ever disruption to global energy supplies, or calmed a parallel war between Israel and Iran-backed Hezbollah in Lebanon nL6N40S0C8. IMPROVING JOB CREATION The World Bank’s governing body, the Development Committee, outlined plans to work with developing countries to streamline policy and regulatory conditions that have hampered investment and job creation for years. Discussions will touch on transparency around permits, anti-corruption, labor law, land law, impediments to opening a business, logistics, better trade systems, and non-price barriers in trade, Banga said. He is upbeat that solutions can be found to help find employment - and dignity - for young people and create opportunities for private companies catering to their needs.  "I don’t know that you can ever get to a situation of utopia and everybody is taken care of in the coming 15 years. I would doubt that’s going to happen, but if you don’t do it, the implications are quite severe in terms of illegal migration and instability," Banga said. United Nations data showed more than 117 million people were displaced worldwide as of 2025. Banga said companies in developing countries themselves were starting to expand globally, including India’s Reliance Industries and the Mahindra Group, and Dangote in Nigeria. Banga said his discussions with officials in developing countries showed their interest in creating more - and better jobs - for the next generation. In addition to jobs, water will be a big focus. The World Bank, in conjunction with other development banks, is set to announce a push to ensure that one billion more people have secure access to clean water, adding to existing initiatives to connect 300 million households in Africa with electricity, and to improve health care. PULLING IN THE PRIVATE SECTOR The World Bank focused on human and physical infrastructure required for the jobs creation push during last fall’s meetings of the IMF and World Bank, and will continue the cycle with an emphasis on attracting private sector investment during this fall’s meetings in Bangkok, Banga said. The bank identified five sectors that would benefit from investment and are not reliant on global trade or outsourcing from developed countries: infrastructure, agriculture for small farmers, primary health care, tourism and value-added manufacturing. Those sectors are less likely to be immediately affected by advancements in artificial intelligence, he said. "The problem is, we can’t do this alone. We’ve got to get this snowball to roll downhill, gathering a lot of snow as it goes along, to reach that amazing number of 800 million," he said.

data.title: Vistry Group stock falls on CEO change
data.description:
Investing.com - Vistry Group PLC (LON:VTYV) shares fell 4.1% on Monday following the announcement that Adam Daniels has been appointed Chief Executive Officer and Executive Director with immediate effect.
Daniels, currently the Executive Chair of one of Vistry’s two largest operating divisions and a member of the Group’s Executive Leadership Team, succeeds Greg Fitzgerald who is stepping down as Executive Chair and CEO by mutual agreement. Rob Woodward will take up his role as Chair and Rowan Baker her role as Senior Independent Director.
The appointment follows a multi-year CEO succession and development process led by the Nomination Committee, supported by external advisers, which considered both internal and external candidates.
[[pro_promotion |
Track the latest on UK stocks with InvestingPro
]]
Daniels joined Countryside Partnerships in 2016 and has held various leadership roles with responsibility for large areas of the Vistry business. Prior to Countryside, he worked at Galliford Try Partnerships and Bloor Homes.
Daniels said the Group is currently focused on improving cash generation, driving Open Market sales and reducing inventory levels. These objectives remain a priority during 2026 to ensure the business is positioned to capture opportunities ahead.
Woodward said the Board is confident that Daniels’ collaborative approach will leverage Vistry’s partnerships capabilities and resources to deliver on the Group’s potential and create long-term value for stakeholders.
Fitzgerald will remain available to assist with transition arrangements. As the Company’s AGM Notice has already been issued, the resolution to re-elect Fitzgerald as a Director is now withdrawn.

data.title: Sterling today: Pound slips as dollar firms on geopolitical tensions
data.description:
Investing.com - Sterling edged lower on Monday, retreating from recent highs as the dollar firmed following renewed geopolitical tensions, with GBP/USD last trading at 1.3416, down 0.3% as of 03:20 ET (07:20 GMT). 
The pound weakened after a strong run last week, as markets reacted to news that the U.S.–Iran peace talks failed to deliver a sustainable ceasefire, dampening risk appetite and supporting the greenback.
[[pro_promotion |
Track real-time FX moves and central bank signals on InvestingPro - up to 50% off
]]
The dollar index rose, underpinned by higher oil prices after Washington signalled a naval blockade on Iranian exports, a move expected to tighten global supply and lift energy costs.
“The dollar has rebounded on failed peace talks in Islamabad, but it doesn’t need to rally too far,” analysts at ING said in a note.
EUR/USD also eased, trading at 1.1689, down 0.3%, although the pair remained above the 1.1600 level. 
ING analysts said the downside was limited, noting that “something like 1.1700 looks a comfortable level for EUR/USD in the near term,” barring another sharp rise in energy prices.
Geopolitics remains the key driver across FX markets, with the Middle East situation continuing to influence both energy prices and broader risk sentiment.
While the latest developments have lent support to the dollar, ING said further gains may be contained. “We doubt the dollar needs to rally too much further,” the bank added.
In the coming weeks, investors will be paying attention to central bank comments from the IMF Spring Meetings, where policymakers are expected to discuss implications of higher oil prices and inflation risks.
In the absence of major data releases, currency markets are likely to remain driven by geopolitical headlines and their impact on energy markets and global risk appetite.

data.title: This is the best semi-cap equipment stock to buy: Wolfe
data.description: A U.S. semiconductor capital equipment maker stands out as the top stock pick among peers as Wolfe Research raised its wafer fab equipment spending forecasts and price targets across the sector, citing clean room constraints that it said will keep supply below demand through 2028.The brokerage raised its CY26 and CY27 WFE estimates to $135 billion and $165 billion, reflecting 22% and 23% year-over-year growth respectively, up from a prior CY26 forecast of 13%. The brokerage rates the sector “market overweight.”Applied Materials Inc. carries an “outperform” rating with a price target raised to $500 from $450, implying 25.7% upside. ASML Holding NV is rated “outperform” with a price target of €1,500, up from €1,450, implying 21.5% upside. KLA Corporation is rated “outperform” with a price target raised to $2,000 from $1,800, implying 15.8% upside. Lam Research Corporation carries a “peer perform” rating with no price target assigned, per Wolfe Research.Applied Materials is the brokerage’s top pick, underpinned by its DRAM exposure, logic spending, and relative valuation. "With AMAT now at 22x that nominal earnings power, we think that’s a strong risk/reward," the analysts said. The analysts model Applied Materials CY26 revenue at $34.1 billion and EPS of $12.30, rising to $41.8 billion and $15.70 in CY27. The $500 price target is based on a CY28 EPS estimate of $18.03, assuming $200 billion in CY28 WFE. AMAT’s current P/E of 28.4x compares with its five-year average of 18x, and sits at an eight-turn discount to SCE peers trading at approximately 33x CY27 consensus EPS.On WFE composition, Wolfe models DRAM up 51% in CY26 and 47% in CY27, Foundry/Logic up 35% and 26% respectively, and NAND up 20% in CY26 and 15% in CY27. China WFE is modeled down approximately 12% in CY26, with ASML guiding China revenue down 35%, while other WFE names guide approximately flat. The brokerage sees six new DRAM fabs required through 2027 to satisfy incremental demand, with clean room availability limiting capacity expansion into CY28.For ASML, CY27 revenue is modeled at €47.1bn with EPS of €39.57, with the €1,500 PT based on 34x a CY28 EPS estimate of €44. For KLA Corporation, CY27 revenue is modeled at $18.5 billion and EPS at $55.62, with the $2,000 PT based on 31x a CY28 EPS estimate of $65. LRCX CY27 estimates stand at $30.1bn revenue and $7.86 EPS.Wolfe noted a low probability of the Match Act driving full DUV restrictions into China, but said the legislation would likely cause Chinese customers to pull in orders, representing potential upside to ASML’s conservative China guidance.

data.title: Baker Hughes to sell Waygate unit to Hexagon for $1.45 bln
data.description:
Investing.com- Baker Hughes Co (NASDAQ:BKR) said on Monday it entered a deal to sell its Waygate Technologies unit to Sweden’s Hexagon (BS:HEXABs) for $1.45 billion in an all-cash transaction. 
The sale is part of a broader restructuring at Baker Hughes, aimed at trimming its overall businesses and improving its cash and capital position. 
[[pro_promotion |Get more breaking news on top U.S. stocks by subscribing to InvestingPro]]
The oil field services provider had earlier this year closed the sales of its Precision Sensors & Instrumentation, and its Cactus Joint Venture units. 
It is also in the process of acquiring Chart Industries for $13.6 billion. 
Baker Hughes said the Waygate sale is expected to close in the second half of 2026. Waygate develops non-destructive testing and inspection technologies that examine materials and components. The unit operates in over 80 countries with over 1,700 employees.

data.title: Oil prices jump above $100/barrel after Trump orders Hormuz blockade
data.description:
Investing.com- Oil prices rose sharply in Asian trade on Monday, surging back above $100 a barrel after U.S. President Donald Trump said the navy will begin blockading the Strait of Hormuz following failed ceasefire talks with Iran. 
Brent oil futures jumped 7.3% to $102.15 a barrel by 22:58 ET (02:58 GMT). Brent had earlier risen as high as $103.87/barrel. 
[[pro_promotion |Get more breaking news on oil and the Iran war by subscribing to InvestingPro]]
US to blockade Hormuz as Iran ceasefire talks fail
The U.S. military will begin enforcing a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and leaving Iranian ports from 10:00 ET (14:00 GMT) on Monday, Central Command said, after Trump ordered a blockade of Hormuz.
Central Command’s statement represented a scaling back from Trump’s earlier threat of blockading all vessels attempting to pass through Hormuz. 
This came after the U.S. and Iran failed to reach a consensus during ceasefire talks held in Pakistan over the weekend. Iran’s nuclear activities, the reopening of Hormuz, and an end to Tehran’s support for proxy groups, including Lebanon’s Hezbollah, were key points of contention. 
Vice President JD Vance, who led the U.S. delegation, left Pakistan early Sunday after 21 hours of negotiations failed to yield an agreement. 
Continued U.S.-Iran hostilities, coupled with a complete blockade of Hormuz, pointed to sustained supply disruptions in oil markets. Iran had effectively blocked Hormuz since the start of the conflict in late-February, cutting off roughly 20% of the world’s oil supply. 
Iran said it had no plans for future nuclear talks with the United States, while Trump said he did not care if Tehran returned to the negotiating table or not. 
But the Wall Street Journal reported that Middle Eastern governments were attempting to broker more ceasefire talks between Washington and Tehran in the coming days.

data.title: U.S. to begin Iran port blockade on April 13, CENTCOM says
data.description:
Investing.com - The U.S. military will begin enforcing a blockade of maritime traffic entering and leaving Iranian ports from 10 AM ET on April 13, under a presidential order aimed at increasing pressure on Tehran.
U.S. Central Command said in a post on X that the measures would apply equally to ships of all nations calling at Iranian ports and coastal areas, including facilities along the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman.
CENTCOM said the operation would not affect freedom of navigation through the Strait of Hormuz for vessels traveling to and from non-Iranian ports. It also said commercial shipping would receive further guidance through a formal notice before the blockade takes effect.
[[pro_promotion |
Get deeper insights on how the Iran war impacts global markets - InvestingPro
]]
6 red lines laid out in Islamabad talks
The United States laid out sweeping demands in talks with Iran in Islamabad, including a full end to uranium enrichment and the dismantling of major nuclear facilities, according to two U.S. officials cited by The Wall Street Journal.
The demands included a complete end to Iran’s uranium enrichment program, the dismantling of all major nuclear enrichment facilities, and the transfer of Iran’s highly enriched nuclear material out of the country.
Washington also insisted on a full reopening of the Strait of Hormuz without tolls, a broader peace arrangement involving regional allies, and an end to Iranian support for proxy groups including Hezbollah in Lebanon and Yemen’s Houthi rebels, the Journal reported.
“We have made very clear what our red lines are, and they have not chosen to accept our terms,” Vice President JD Vance said after the talks, according to the Journal.
Vance left Pakistan early Sunday without a deal after what U.S. officials had expected to be a short initial meeting turned into 21 hours of negotiations. The talks were the highest-level direct engagement between Washington and Tehran since the 1979 Iranian revolution. 
Trump orders blockage of the Strait of Hormuz
After high-stakes peace negotiations between the U.S. and Iran broke down in Islamabad overnight amid disagreements over Iran’s nuclear ambitions, U.S. President Donald Trump announced on Sunday that the U.S. Navy will begin blockading all ships from entering or leaving the Strait of Hormuz.
"Effective immediately, the United States Navy, the Finest in the World, will begin the process of BLOCKADING any and all Ships trying to enter, or leave, the Strait of Hormuz," Trump stated. "At some point, we will reach an “ALL BEING ALLOWED TO GO IN, ALL BEING ALLOWED TO GO OUT” basis, but Iran has not allowed that to happen by merely saying, “There may be a mine out there somewhere,” that nobody knows about but them. THIS IS WORLD EXTORTION, and Leaders of Countries, especially the United States of America, will never be extorted."
Trump added that the U.S. Navy will seek and interdict every vessel in International Waters that has paid a toll to Iran and will start the process of destroying the mines the Iranians laid in the Straits.
"Any Iranian who fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be BLOWN TO HELL!," Trump stated.  In a separate post, Trump said, " IRAN WILL NEVER HAVE A NUCLEAR WEAPON!"
The comments from Trump set up the potential for an escalation of hostilities. Following the failed meeting in Islamabad, Iran said that it will prevent safe passage through the Strait until the U.S. agrees to a deal. “Iran is not in a hurry, and until the U.S. agrees to a reasonable deal, there will be no change in the situation of the Strait of Hormuz,” said the Fars News Agency, quoting an unnamed official.
"Despite various initiatives from the Iranian delegation, the unreasonable demands of the American side prevented the progress of the negotiations. Thus, the negotiations ended," IRIB said on Telegram.
The Islamic Republic said it has no plans for further nuclear talks with the U.S. after negotiations brokered by Pakistan failed. “No program has yet been announced for the time, place, or next round of negotiations,” said Nour, Iran’s Supreme National Security Council’s news agency.
Trump signals "win" as military readiness ramps up
As the diplomatic channel closed, President Trump played down the significance of the breakdown, asserting that the U.S. holds the strategic upper hand. “Whether we make a deal or not makes no difference to me,” Trump said, adding, “And the reason is because we’ve won.” 
Despite the positive rhetoric, the administration is preparing for a potential return to hostilities. Trump noted that the U.S. is currently "loading up ships" with ammunition and weaponry in the event the ceasefire collapses entirely.
The naval theater remains a flashpoint. While the U.S. confirmed that two destroyers successfully transited the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, the first such move since the war began, the IRGC Navy has publicly denied the transit occurred. 
Iranian officials warned that any attempt to challenge their control of the waterway would be met with a “firm and forceful response.” 
The exit of the U.S. delegation without a "Safe Passage" agreement for energy exports suggests that the Hormuz blockade and its associated "war premium" on oil and shipping are likely to persist.
(Frank DeMatteo and Senad Karaahmetovic contributed to this report)
